sentences.text: Well, first the setting up of the homes, as I said, they were more or less transferred, the homes.
And then they began from themselves, to organize life.
And it became eventually very, very organized, particularly under Josef Rosensaft.
Whatever else he was, was a wonderful organizer, a very powerful character.
And his name is very much remembered by the survivors.
He is no longer alive, but his son carries on a foundation helping survivors.
And, and they settled down to pre-- normal life.
The army was supplying food, very nice standard.
And the hospital was there in the new camp.
And they began to come back to life.
And little-- these little groups kept together.
But socially, many things were organized-- slowly of course-- as I said, schools, study circles, dance circles, and all sorts of things--
--like that which are in a normal life, until eventually, as I said, they-- and they-- under Rosensaft, they, they organized a police force, their own police force, to police the camp.
Of course, there was an awful lot of fenanicking, if you know what that word means.
And this is the origin of what is called protectsia.
I don't know if you've heard the word.
Protectsia means protection.
And it wasn't what you knew, but who you knew if you wanted to get something extra, some little luxury.
And there was a tremendous amount of bartering and, and so on.
And everything wasn't idyllic.
But on the whole, it became, became a fairly normal society, living, of course, under very strange conditions.
Oh, oh, I don't remember.
Many thousands.
Many thousands.
So it was quite a--
Must have been 15,000, 20,000 people.
It was quite a, a, a, an undertaking.
It wasn't just a--
Tremendous.
Tremendous.
No, they weren't governed by-- as far as internally is concerned, the British were only too pleased to allow them to organize themselves.

And they-- there were many-- you've got to differentiate between the official authorities and the man, the soldier in the street, the officer in the street, as you might say, many of whom were highly sympathetic and helped them a great deal with materials and things like that.
So they gradually became an organized, reasonably organized, community.
As I said, Rosensaft eventually became the chief and, and had a sort of council that worked with him.
And, and this is the way life was.
It was a strange kind of society, but it was a society.
Rabbi, you-- were all of the community Jewish at this barracks?
They were all Jews.
Oh, no.
No.
No.
There were still non-- still thousands of non-Jews as well.
Yes.
So were still non-Jews.
So--
But they were gradually being sent away because they had no answer when they said they didn't want to go.

Because many of them didn't want to go because they faced trouble from the Russians, and the Lithuanians, and so on who had themselves-- many of whom had been with the Nazis in persecuting the Jews and others-- didn't want to go back to Lithuania and to other parts of that part of the w-- Eastern Europe.
But they were forced to go back by the Russians, by Stalin's command and demands from the Allies.
And many of them met a very, very sticky end.
Right.
Yes, well, now, politics-- politics played a very important part, British army-- British politics, not army politics.
The-- as I said, the people of other nationalities were being sent back.
The Jews refused to go back to the places from whence they had come.
And they demanded-- the official Jewish organizations demanded that the Jews should be kept together and eventually be allowed to go to the land of Israel, which was at that time called Palestine.
This was the aim of official jury.
And the British wanted the opposite.
The British-- no, the British government.
Not the Army, the British government.
The Army had no say in this matter, only to carry out their orders.
The British government wanted to break up the strength of the camp so-- and to keep the land of Palestine, keep it closed.
They allowed certain numbers, but a very handful as compared to those who had-- who needed to go and wanted to go.
So one fine day, they came to us-- to myself and Jane and Abraham, the two-- my two helpers-- and said, look.
We are going to establish a Jewish camp.
We'll take the Jews away from Bergen-Belsen, leave the other nationalities.
We'll take them away.
We'll establish a Jewish camp until the political problems have been solved.
Well, this seemed to be what we'd been asking.
So we did-- we had to go along with it in any case.
Who were we three-- we had to go along with it.
But we tried to make the best of it.
And I shall never forget they came, and they said, we're taking 2,000 people in the first batch.
So they brought tremendous lorries.
And against many protests from the people, who-- some of whom might have been, been taken away from people they liked or what and when-- they were-- was very mixed feeling.
They didn't know where they were going.
Neither did we.
But we were told they're being taken to a very nice camp where there'd be only Jews.
So with great difficulty, we loaded these people onto the trucks.
And we started off early in the morning.
I was driving-- I was not driving.
I was sitting in the cab of one of the things, and the other two in other cabs.
And we started.
Now, it was a long, long distance, a long distance.
It-- altogether it took us about 10 hours to get from Bergen-Belsen to this camp which was called Diepholz.
It was not too far from a place called Osnabruck in northern Germany.
And it was a terrible journey because the roads were very bad.
They'd been blown up and blasted and so on.
So there were many times where we had to take too long detours through fields and so on.
And the people were not in the best of health for a job like that.

And, and, of course, from time to time, they had to stop to allow people to carry out their natural functions and so on, which they had to do in the fields, and-- And I'll never forget one little incident, which I think I must speak about.
We stopped at a little hamlet, what one might call it.
And the people got off the, the lorries.
And just nearby was a little market garden kind of place, you know, a big garden with chickens running around and so on.
Well, they just ran into these little farms and started pulling out the vegetables and grabbing the chickens and so on.
The officer in command of the whole expedition was a lieutenant.
And he tried to create order.
And somebody had grabbed a chicken, and he came and grabbed the other side.
And he said, leave, girl.
And he actually pulled out his revolver.
He wanted to try and get order.
Well, it was wrong.
But at the same time, one can understand the feeling of these people.
They are Germans who tortured us.
Why must they have chickens?
We want the chickens.
Fortunately, I had one pip more than him.
So I ordered him to put his, his revolver away.
And anyway, we let the chicken go.
[LAUGHTER] That was a very surprised chicken.
And, and back we went onto our journey.
We arrived after dark.
And when we got there, we were horror-stricken.
They were old barracks with broken windows, not much better than the original Bergen-Belsen.
And the main part of the camp had thousands and thousands of Russians, and Latvians, and a whole mix of other people.
But we were there.
It was dark.
We moved in.
The people slept on the floor the first night or so.
The next morning, we started to try and organize something.
We got in-- we first-- my first duty was to communicate with London, and to say what had happened, and to demand that they be taken back again to Bergen-Belsen.
But meanwhile, they were there.
So we started to organize.
And I must say, we organize pretty well.
The commandant of the camp provided us with all-- with a kitchen, you know, for-- to, to feed the people, and with supplies.
And I had to appoint cooks and such like people in charge of the kitchen.
You can imagine there was tremendous rivalry for that post, because those in the kitchen could always manage a little bit more.
And so-- and what they didn't eat themselves, they could use for protectsia and such like things.
But we got settled down.
It was-- it was near a very small village.
We got settled down.
We had a very nice Canadian chap who was the commandant of the whole camp.
And, and they were reasonably well looked after.
And we, Abraham and I, started trying to interest the people more in observing things.
We went around to all the churches.
We had no candles.
And we went around, and we borrowed candles the first Friday we were there.
And we cut them up so that-- and encouraged the people to light Shabbat candles.
And life went on there for quite some time.

And eventually, of course, what happened was that the civilian relief workers, the Jewish civilian relief team from London, which was waiting and had been waiting for months and months because no civilians were allowed in, were at last allowed into the camp, to come.
And so they-- part of that group took over from us.
And not so long after that, the whole camp was taken back again to Bergen-Belsen.
To the Panzer barracks.
To the Panzer barracks.
Yeah.
So it was just the 2,000 that stayed there.
No more came--
No more came.
No.
No.
We stopped.
We said no more will come.
I don't know.
[LAUGHS]
Well, they were, by then--
No.
There were no other chaplains at all.
But they were-- by that time, they were well organized.
They didn't really need us.
As I said, Rosensaft was a wonderful organizer.
And the camp was organized.
They had their own police and so on.
So the social structures had already been set up there.
The social structures went on.
And the-- and the political aspect was being dealt with mainly from London and-- from Israel and from London.
We were at-- I was at Diepholz for about a month, a month, six weeks, or so.
And when the civilians came in, then I--
--returned to military duties.
No, no, no.
No, no.
I was posted to a place called Iserlohn, in the rural area.
And then I, I travelled around the whole area, an area about half the size of [?
Natale, ?] wherever Jewish boys were stationed.
And I-- at various centers, I used to organize Shabbat Eve services, Shabbat morning services, and Shabbat afternoon services and keep the boys in touch with Judaism.
And the first Rosh Hashanah New Year services I held in a barracks at a place called Munster, M-U-N-S-T-E-R, quite a large town.
And we had the-- about 400 soldiers, Jewish soldiers, attended that service because they were still-- the army hadn't started really heavily demobilizing.
And I remember that--
1945, Rosh Hashanah, 1945.
And I remember the-- the-- this big hall where we held it was part of the barracks, but it was also the only sort of communal hall left.
And we had put up big blue and white flags, you know, over the platform.
And that very evening, there-- after the end of Rosh Hashanah, there was a big musical concert, a German, you know-- organized.
And we left the flags all in place for them to see.
No.
I was involved with the army.
But unofficially we knew what was going on.
But officially we didn't know.
Because those people, many, many people, A, from Bergen-Belsen and other camps.
And B, people who were coming from deep in Russia through Germany, through Belgium-- through Berlin, rather-- through Berlin, into Germany, into the British zone, particularly.
And from there, filtering down to Italy or over the borders into France to Marseille, and getting on what was called Aliyah Bet, the illegal-- the illegal immigration into Israel.
And there were many, many Israelis from the Jewish Brigade who, for quite some time, were stationed in Belgium who were roving around the country helping these people in many ways.
All over Germany, and penetrating into Russia and all over.
It is really remarkable what those boys did in bringing and encouraging people to come through.
And it was here that I had a very unusual experience.
Shall I tell you about it now?
My office was in a house in Germany, in Iserlohn, where all the, the-- where the senior chaplains of each religion had their offices.
And the senior chaplain of us all, the official officer in charge of the chaplains, was the, the Protestant bishop, whatever he was.
And I had my office on the ground floor.
One day, I come to my office, and I find a note from a Christian chaplain saying that there were a group of Jews in a camp at-- and gave me the map references.
I couldn't understand this, because when I looked it up, it looked like an internment camp for Nazi criminals.
How can Jews get there?
So together with my faithful batman, Abey, we started off.
We went to this place.
And when we got there, I entered the camp and reported to the adjutant's office, and-- a major.
And he said, padre, I shouldn't be doing this, but I asked the chaplain to let you know.
We have 40 or 50 young Jews whose only crime is they were trying to get across the border to get into Europe and to eventually join into illegal immigration to go to the land of Israel.
He said, we have to stop them, but I don't think this was the place in which to bring them.
This is a Nazi criminal camp for Nazi criminals and not for them.
So he took me into the camp.
He took me to them, and I got them together.
And they were, of course, overjoyed to see a Jew, or a Jewish officer.
And we davened, we prayed.
We had the afternoon and evening service.
And I promised them that within a few days, they would be out.
The next-- I went back to my camp.
And the next morning I went up to the headquarters, and I asked to see the brigadier.
Well, as a chaplain, I suppose he thought I'd come to discuss some religious matter with him.
So I got admission.
And I told him in no uncertain terms that this was the wrong thing and that I was reporting to headquarter-- to my headquarters in London.
And if something was not done, it would be publicized all over the world.
I said, I want these people out of that camp immediately.
We can take them to Bergen-Belsen, but not in that camp.
So he immediately-- I must say, he immediately gave the order.
And I went then to the Jewish relief organization, which had-- their vans all had big Magen Davids on.
They were the equivalent of Red Cross kind of thing.
And the next day, we rode through all these Nazi, Nazis, and loaded up these people, and took them to Bergen-Belsen.
One interesting thing was that as I walked through the camp on the first occasion, a middle-aged man clicked his heels and sort of saluted me.
And the major told me that's Krupp, K-R-U-P-P, the head of the Krupp Organization, the-- who supplied the armaments for the Germans and so on.
So that was my-- I didn't speak to him or anything.
I had very little contact with Germans.
Some contact.

In Iserlohn, I was able to help a Jewish family which had returned from Theresienstadt, when the people who'd moved into their flat had taken away their nice stove-- they call it a [NON-ENGLISH], that kind of a stove with a big long pipe that they had-- had taken theirs when they were forced to move out and give them back their home and put in an inferior one.
So I went to the mayor, and I nearly, you know-- oh, no.
No.
Me?
I was never a Nazi.
I had a great-great-grandfather who was-- the great-mother who-- you know, the usual thing.
No, but only one man ever admitted to me that he was a Nazi, and he was a manufacturer of cutlery or something.
And-- but otherwise, no.
None of them were really Nazis.
No, no.
No, no.
It was about a four or five hours' drive.
Oh, do you know where it was?
No.
Can't remember.
Can't remember.
[LAUGHTER] I could look up the map.
It was in the-- it wasn't too far from Bergen-Belsen, I think.
So they took them back to Bergen-Belsen.
And from there, they infiltrated again.
I don't know.
I take it they did.
Well, everybody did in '48, of course.
Those who were in the DP camps and those who were in Cyprus and so on.
Yes.

Well now, another aspect of the illegal immigration-- we call it Aliyah Bet-- was carried out by my friend-- my dear friend Abraham, who, as I said, spoke French fluently as well as German and Yiddish.
And he wasn't too bad in English.
And he was quite a modern rabbi.
And we received-- some nut in England got the idea of providing the chaplains with a big vehicle, tremendous sort of caravan type of vehicle, so that the chaplain could take his synagogue with him.
Well, I had a-- I had a little ark made for me in the work-- in the army workshop.
It was a small Sefer Torah.
And I had a 1,500 weight lor-- van.
And that was quite enough.
So they sent these great big, lumbering things.
And nobody knew what to do with them.

By this time, Abraham had been adopted by the Joint, the American Joint Distribution Committee, which had an office in Brunswick, which is more near the border-- near the British zone bordered with, with the, with the Russian zone.
And in Brunswick, there was also a very, very fine Jewish, Jewish kind of [INAUDIBLE], a Jewish what would you call it?
Army assistance group?
No.
It was a-- not a hotel.
A club.
Oh, a club.
OK.
Sorry, OK.
A Jewish club open to everybody during the week, but closed on Friday night and Saturday, when we held Jewish services there, you see.
We had-- always had a nice Friday night supper and so on and so forth.
And the, the Joint had a brunch there as well.
And they adopted Abraham, and he adopted one of these vans, which nobody had any use for.
And what he used to do was to pack as many people as he could into the van, take a lot of prayer books along as well, and he would drive through into France.
He already had the-- many of the border guards were already sort of being smoothed over.
And as he spoke French and one thing and another, if he was stopped by somebody who didn't know, he was going to a service.
This was-- look.
There's a Magen David, and there's all this.
And so he carried on this activity for many, many months, ferrying people out of Germany down to Marseille.
And, and there, they waited their turn to try and get onto the small boats and so on that were, were carrying out this duty.
Oh.
I didn't know, but I knew.
That Rosh Hashanah, the second Rosh-- my second Rosh Hashanah, 1946 now-- I had-- by that time, the Jewish soldiers, thousands had been demobbed.
So my congregation had shrunk very, very much.
And so I was able to hold my High Holy Day services in the Jewish club in Brunswick.
I was no longer stationed in Iserlohn.
I'd be moved to Luneberg, well known place.
Now-- another half hour?
Mhm.
And, and on Yom Kippur in afternoon, the club was closed of course, and we were holding services.
And Abraham was assisting me.
And there was a banging on the door.
And somebody went, and they came up and said, the military police would like to see me.
So I took off my tallit, and I went down to see what it was all about.
And they said they want to see Abraham Goldfinger.
And, and they wanted to interview him and see what was going on.
So I said, look.
This is the most holy day of our year.
We're having a service.
You can't see him now.
You can't disturb our service.
I promise you I will bring him to your office tomorrow.
So they went, and we continued with the service.
And poor Abraham of course was trembling.
The next day, I took him to the officers, and they grilled him as to what he was up to and so on.
And he came out back, and he was trembling.
We decided it was time for him to go.
And so we packed him off to Bergen-Belsen to join the illegal immigration himself.
